item.dc:creator: loadingartist

item.title: Well, Heck
item.description: Mass on my brain. Right now, don't know what it is, how it got there, or what we're going to do about it. Laying in the hospital and waiting. I am not afraid of dying, death does not scare me at all. But I have a severely autistic little boy who needs his mama, leaving him is simply not an option. UPDATE: Five minutes afteri posted, in comes the doc. Surgery in a week, staying here on steroids til then to keep the swelling down. It's not in my brain, in front of it and they think they can get it all. Really really good news.
item.dc:creator: hahway

item.title: Images of Note: Cold Elmo
item.description:
Oh come now, clowns are funny
replace that duck with a cat and you get how a lot of people talk about their cats. incidentally, Eric is a criminal.
(This, incidentally, is why you cannot send me shit).
F E E T
LSD
"Remembering Bombay's new name is sooooooo hard" "I'm going to force cities to use the names I know them by using the law."
America: Land of the Free, unless you wanna protest the government in which case it's legal to kill you.
This is why FNV is the best game.
Hell Yeah!
Prison? Sounds more like heaven.
AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
:3
Mood
For Chats: http://discord.abbii.gay For Shitposts: http://tumblr.abbii.gay For srs and maybe shitposts: http://mastodon.abbii.gay Also STREAMING TONIGHT, COME STARE AT ME! http://twitch.tv/abbiistabbii
item.dc:creator: abbiistabbii

item.title: If you make a nostril sound, you have to hit the green arrow thingy (or don't I'm not your mom)

1
2. Been there....
3. MF what are you doing?!
4
5. Just roll x 100 000
6. Also goodbye legs
7
8
9
10. Humans be weird
11. What else feels like this?
12
13
14
15. Literally me
item.dc:creator: TheseusGames

item.title: Dump of random Friday mames (no politics)
item.description:
1. Why are BMW drivers the same in every country?
15. Me trying to complete a project for once
2. We see trough your lies
3
4. Girl attempts gravity
5
6. Bro, I found your drone
7
8. We can be friends, please. Literally message me :/
9
10
11
12
13. Where is your Giraffe peasants
14. Hey friendos If you liked the humor of this dump I think you might like my wacky "Stanley Parable"-inspired game, called "Do Not Press The Button (To Delete The Multiverse)"! Please wishlist, it would really encourage me to finish it: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Dont_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse_The_Game/ P. S. Have a great Friday and awesome weekend and don't forget to hydrate and take care of yourself. Go on a hike if the weather is nice : )
item.dc:creator: TheseusGames

item.title: SecretHarpy's Meme Library: Volume 25
item.description:
Hello my dear imgur friends, welcome to another meme dump. Imgur ate my post the last time I attempted to upload, so let's try this again. ONCE MORE, WITH FEELING
bladder why you like this
every goddamn time. I WANNA KNOW WHAT HAPPENS.
My entire life my mom has been saying stuff like this. It was right there with the whole "This goth stuff is just a phase, you'll grow out of it." Well, jokes on you mom. I'm in my 30's now and I'm even MORE of a goth liberal woke snowflake than I was before! Take that!
It really is, but death science is super neato
Wonder what he's up to these days
My dog is 25 pounds but he takes up the space of an entire human being, somehow
The only correct choice
Yeah, no thanks to that. I'd rather live where the air hurts my face
free sody
Man I sure wish I knew
hell yeah marathon time
Potatoes really are amazing
I probably fell off rainbow road. Again.
That's all for this dump folks, I'll see you in the next one. Until then, stay awesome
item.dc:creator: SecretHarpy

item.title: Trying to fall asleep while knowing someone 3 hours away turned a rare National Cash Register wooden base into a cheap Etsy flip.
item.description:
Found this for sale one state over. A three hour drive one way. I'm tempted if for no other reason than to undo the dorm room "glow up" and find a register for it.
Worst part is, without a register installed on top the drawers won't open. Not without manually manipulating a mechanism hidden beneath the fake plants they put on it. It's effectively been turned into a shelf. On stilts. Remember, kids. Friends don't let friends live-laugh-love.
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: One year of doing Funny Friday. A dump of my favorites (video heavy)
item.description:
A little over a year ago I started making these posts that I call Funny Friday, where I'd put together a collection of things I found amusing and share it here on imgur. Over the course of 20+ posts I've gotten a lot of great feedback and support on these posts (surprisingly enough, considering my weird sense of humor) and thought I'd put together a collection of some of my favorites to celebrate a year of these. Like I've been doing lately, the raunchier stuff is in a private folder, and I'll put a link to it in the comments. I think there's a lot of good material there, so I hope you enjoy! (Usually I make smartass comments about the different stuff in my dumps, but this time I thought I'd spare everyone me either repeating past stuff or struggling to come up with something new)
Hope you're having a good Friday and it leads into a great weekend.
item.dc:creator: PoliticalWanderer

And ended up making a meme. Passengers carried in rail transport per year. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_usage
item.dc:creator: NotSteveWozniak

item.title: More than 36K Georgians claimed tax exemptions for ‘unborn dependents’
item.description: More than 36,000 Georgians used a new “unborn dependents” deduction in 2022, lowering their taxable income by about $109 million, state officials say. The new deduction is the result of Georgia’s 2019 abortion law, which allows expectant parents to claim an embryo or fetus as a dependent on their taxes. Georgia is the only state known to have that kind of deduction. The exemption for 2022 was worth $3,000 per embryo or fetus, as it is for other minor dependents, which results in about a $170 tax benefit. Only people who were pregnant by or after July 20, 2022, when Georgia’s law took effect, were eligible for the tax break. The General Assembly passed legislation earlier this year that would increase the per-child deduction to $4,000. The state Department of Revenue does not require filers to provide proof of a pregnancy up front to take the exemption, as is the case of most other exemptions, although the filer would have to provide it if audited. Georgia’s law took effect a few weeks after the U.S. Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade, the nearly 50-year-old decision that guaranteed a nationwide right to abortion. Georgia law bans most abortions once a doctor can detect fetal cardiac activity, which is typically about six weeks into a pregnancy and before many know they are pregnant. Guidelines from the Department of Revenue encouraged Georgians to use the “other deductions” line on tax forms to itemize “UNBORNDEP,” but since the directive was not required, state revenue officials said filers used a variety of terms to indicate their intent to use the unborn dependent exemption. That made it difficult for the department to determine how many Georgians took advantage of the benefit until earlier this year, agency officials said. Auxier said Georgia’s approach does not ultimately give meaningful tax relief to expectant parents and offered that instead, the state should pass a child tax credit, similar to the ones of 15 other states. Credits, he said, are more valuable to taxpayers. “Credit is different than a deduction,” Auxier said. “A deduction lowers your taxable income, but a credit lowers your tax bill. ... There is this very simple and effective way to support mothers of young children, and it is sitting right there if they want to do it. But they’re choosing this.” What is and isn’t allowed under the ‘unborn dependent exemption’ Q: Do I need to include proof of pregnancy? A: No. Q: If I get pregnant and give birth in the same tax year, can I claim both the unborn dependent exemption and the traditional dependent exemption? A: No. Q: If my pregnancy progresses to the point where fetal cardiac activity is detected but I have a miscarriage, can I still claim the exemption? A: Yes. Q: If I have multiple pregnancies that reach the point of detectable fetal cardiac activity in a tax year, can I claim each pregnancy? A: Yes. Q: How much is the unborn dependent exemption? A: The same as other minor dependents — a $3,000* deduction on reported income, which results in about $170 in a tax benefit. https://www.ajc.com/politics/georgians-claimed-more-than-109-million-in-deductions-for-unborn-dependents/6KGBN2VLCBADDPHVQQYM4BJG6Q/
item.dc:creator: Jbelkin

item.title: Food Waste Rescue Squad | Urban Gleaners
item.description: Urban Gleaners is a Portland, Oregon-based food waste rescue squad that collects fresh food before it can go to waste – then redistributes it to people in need. Formed in 2006, the nonprofit believes that ugly is good enough.
item.dc:creator: 60SecondDocs

item.title: Post-launch
item.description:
A little while ago, I wrote here about my new book, which grew out of a series of articles I wrote here, concerning the African-American "Colonial Marines". These African-Americans, during the War of 1812 (1812-1815), fled from American slaveowners to find their freedom with raiding British forces. Amongst those refugees, roughly 1000 men became a unique military force in British history - the "Colonial Marines" - part of the Royal Marines. This radically changed the British attacks on the American coasts of Virginia, Maryland and Georgia, and is a forgotten reason for the ability of a small British force, with no cavalry and limited artillery, to advance inland and burn Washington DC. But the story doesn't end there, as the war came to an inconclusive end, British leaders were considering creating, arming and supporting a 'black Republic' on the territory of the US (most likely Georgia) only terminated by the sudden end of the war. And in north Florida (then Spanish), very briefly, a unique 'statelet' briefly appeared that may have changed the whole trajectory of North American History. I launched this book at London's National Army Museum in early April & this is the tale of that, and what incredible links writing can bring to you.
[Picture: your author totally not bricking it in front of a crowd.] The launch was a fantastic bit of fun, I had a much larger crowd than I anticipated with dozens of friends and colleagues showing their support alongside many interested members of the National Army Museum's public. Amidst them I was lucky enough to meet Kandace Chimbiri, a children's author who focuses particularly on black British history - I highly recommend her "The Story of Britain's Black Airmen" - like many Britons, the 1969 "Battle of Britain" film aligned with the near-mythological status of that stand against fascism ensures that the sound of a Merlin engined Spitfire touches my soul, though my mind loves the good old Hurricane! The reaction of many people in the crowd, from looks of horror, to laughs, and gasps, as I told a tale that must, by its nature, take in the depravity of slavery, the joys of liberation, and the struggles of those who dared to be free, was incredible and powerful. To be the conduit by which historical events can be experienced all over again by the power of my words was a privilege I scarcely feel qualified for.
[Picture: One of Ms Chimbiri's books, reminding us importantly that even when Britain "stood alone", it was standing alone as the head of a multi-ethnic Empire without which resistance to a continental juggernaught would have been impossible.] I also got to speak at some length about the descendents of the Colonial Marines, who are spread about forming a great proportion of Nova Scotian Afro-Canadians, to some of the Afro-Seminole of both south central Florida & those of Andros Island in the Bahamas, to their most obvious legacy, the Merikin people of Trinidad and Tobago.
[Picture: me talking about the Merikin's proud legacy, illustrated with a picture from 2019's Trinidad & Tobago Carnival, in which young Merikins paraded in replica redcoats.] The Merikins are a nearly unique Afro-Caribbean group, alongside the people of Haiti, they are part of the African diaspora that directly fought for their freedom and dignity as human beings, and secured it from a slave-holding Republic by their military service - albeit in the service of what was, with a deep irony and hypocrisy I enjoyed illustrating to the crowd, at that time still a slave-holding Empire. I have had the pleasure of making contact with the Merikin Commission, one of a number of community groups on Trinidad representing the Merikins. Currently the group is involved in discussion with Trinidad's Ministry of Education to have the arrival of the Merikins included in the Primary age education syllabus of Trinidad & Tobago, as part of securing the legacy of the Merikins.
[Picture: your author with Mr C McNish of the Merikin Commission, the great-great-great-great-great-great-grandson of a series of McNishs who escaped from slavery in the state of Georgia, fought as part of the Royal Navy's Colonial Marines, and settled as free people on Trinidad in 1816.] Originally an insular and somewhat isolated community, which preserved their culture, modern Merikins are an increasingly mobile and diffused group concerned that their unique heritage may evaporate from purely social memory. This is perhaps somewhat ironically illustrated by one of the Merikin Commission's leaders being now based in Maryland, a state of the United States from which their ancestors fled! I am hopeful that in the future my 'little work' will be part of securing the Merikin legacy, and am preparing myself now to provide what services I can to that end. On top of this lovely Caribbean connection, a close friend put me in touch with a whole series of US military archivists and researchers with an array of impressively scary email address (.pentagon, .DoD, etc) who kindly helped me in my research.
[Picture: What an honour!] Unexpectedly, this led to the above, which has led to my book - which started as a post on Imgur I remind you! - entering the library of the US Army Center of Military History. The book has also been entered into the library of the UK Parliament's House of Lords, joining an article I had published in the Royal Marines' "Globe and Laurel" magazine - which was a rewrite and merging of two articles I wrote here on Imgur. But the adventure did not end there, while I was researching - deep in the trenches of piling through primary sources and trying to construct timelines I had, as most writers do, a moment of 'why do I bother' and 'who will ever read this' that stopped me writing for a few days, by serendipity when I returned to research I fell across a 2016 article by a US Army Chaplain, concerning the Colonial Marines.
[Picture: The article which bucked me right up.] This article was a godsend, reminding me that the story I was trying to tell could - and would - mean something to those who learnt of it. Thanks to the links with all these American military types who'd helped with research, I cheekily asked if I could be put in contact with this gentleman, and after only a few days (& some permission-seeking) I was shocked to receive contact details! I subsequently had a delightful call with the now retired Chaplain-Major, and am looking forward to meeting him during a visit to the United Kingdom in the near future.
[Picture: me having fun with flags ... my idea for a "Merikin community" flag to replace the one currently in service, six stripes for the six companies of Colonial Marines at disbanding, four stars for the four (now) American states they fought in - Maryland, Virginia, Florida and Georgia.] During my meeting with Mr McNish of the Merikin Commission, he mentioned that the current Merikin community flag, an African tricolour emblazoned with "Merikin 1816" and crossed muskets, is currently in line for a potential redesign, which fired up my nerdy vexillologist mind. However, there was a much greater artistic mind at play around my book. I spoke about the book on LinkedIn (the work facebook) and made the acquaintence of the well-renowned military artist Jason Askew - his work appears in lots of Regimental museums and other military contexts, but he's also done some beautiful Star Wars art(!). I had a coffee with Jason and told the story of the Colonial Marines; he was captured by it, he'd previously worked on the subject of African-Americans in the American Revolution, and produced a Juneteenth painting for the US Embassy in London.
[Picture: the US Embassy in the UK's official Twitter ("X") account posts about Jason Askew's fine work.] With incredible speed and skill, Jason Askew painted an image of the Colonial Marines going into action - capturing a moment that may have occured at Bladensburg, North Point, or down in Georgia. He turned this painting around so fast it was on display during the launch, and became a 'slide' in the presentation, a role it will likely continue to play in future events I do - at least until someone buys it! On top of all this, I was directed by a kind person in a Napoleonic Wargaming focused Facebook group to Chris Parkinson of Redcoat History, a podcast & youtube channel devoted to British military history. Chris (who has great experience with documentary work) did an incredibly skilled interview with me, drawing out the story brilliantly, despite my "Mitchell and Webb historian" style of flailing my arms about.
[Picture: your author being completely overwhelmed by the amazing art of Jason Askew. The cover of my book features a Colonial Marine by the excellent Don Troiani, integrated into a design I dreamed up with a terrible MS paint collage featuring the flag of Trinidad and Tobago, the US flag (of 1812) and the Union Jack, but made real by the work of Jon Wilkinson, a cover designer for Pen & Sword books.] This has all been such a roller coaster of joy. History is fascinating because it is the story of people, who are always interesting. I feel a great privilege at being told that I have, out of a motivation I'd find hard to name, revealed and secured a precious part of the human experience for posterity. And it all began with writing little articles on Imgur on the weekend. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ One person at the launch made a charming instagram post about it: https://www.instagram.com/reel/C5dguGWNCMc/ Redcoat History's interview with me, in which I flail my arms about a lot, and had a long pre-recording debate with myself about which Sabaton shirt to wear: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jae88yaVCb8&t=927s And, of course, my book: UK Amazon: https://www.amazon.co.uk/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014 US Amazon: https://www.amazon.com/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII

item.dc:creator: lunarcatsss
